input.background: A manufacturer of paper products wants to compare the variation in daily production levels at two paper mills. Independent random samples of days are selected from each mill, and the production levels (in units) are recorded. The data are shown in Table 8.8. The goal is to determine whether there is sufficient evidence to indicate a difference in the variability of production levels at the two paper mills. The significance level is $\alpha=.10$.
input.data:
\begin{tabular}{l|l|l|l|l|l|l|l|l}
\hline Table 8.8 & \multicolumn{9}{|l}{ Production Levels at Two Paper Mills } \\
\hline Mill 1: & 34 & 18 & 28 & 21 & 40 & 23 & 29 & \\
& 25 & 10 & 38 & 32 & 22 & 22 & & \\
\hline Mill 2: & 31 & 13 & 27 & 19 & 22 & 18 & 23 & 21\\
& 18 & 15 & 24 & 13 & 19 & 18 & 23 & 13\\
\hline
\end{tabular}
input.question: What conclusion can be drawn from the test statistic and the rejection region?

output.answer: The calculated value of $F=2.97$ falls in the rejection region (greater than $F_{05}=2.38$). Therefore, the data provide sufficient evidence to indicate that the population variances differ. It appears that the variation in production levels at mill 1 tends to be higher than the variation at mill 2.
